scope: Domain charters and the requirement-authoring standard: the TAXONOMY.md charter doc, the per-envelope scope field, and the CLI surfaces (spec domain list --json, spec req) that carry a domain's charter to the author at authoring time. Belongs here: what tells an author whether a requirement belongs in a domain. Does not belong here: the requirement lifecycle mechanics themselves (spec req/supersede/amend → REQ) or the domain scaffold (spec domain new → DOMAIN); this domain owns the charter, not the plumbing.

requirements.statement: spec-engine/TAXONOMY.md carries one charter entry per target domain — a scope sentence plus a belongs-here / does-not-belong-here list with at least one named boundary case — together with the 3-digit requirement-id rule and the shadow-id promotion backlog, when a domain exists in the target taxonomy.
requirements.why: Without a written charter an author cannot tell whether a new requirement belongs in a domain, so the taxonomy silently re-rots after every reorg.

requirements.statement: spec-engine/TAXONOMY.md states the requirement-authoring standard — the statement is the input→output promise of the GUARD template, verifying tags are the validating tests, livesIn plus implementing tags are the realizing files, and issue links are provenance only — when a requirement is authored.
requirements.why: Cold-read failures get written exactly when there is no standard to author against; the anatomy triple keeps tests, files, and provenance from being mistaken for identity.

requirements.statement: The shared SpecDomainSchema carries an optional scope that round-trips through validateAndWrite→orderDomain, so the domain charter travels on every SPEC.json envelope, when a domain is written and re-read.
requirements.why: If the charter cannot ride on the envelope it exists only in one repo's doc, and every adopter loses the placement guidance that keeps requirements in the right domain.

requirements.statement: spec domain list --json emits each domain's scope in a key-sorted array of {key, scope} objects — null when a domain has no scope and [] when the platform has none — when the domain listing is requested.
requirements.why: A charter the read surface cannot show never reaches the author at the moment of placement, so the scope field would pay no rent.

requirements.statement: spec req <domain> prints the resolved domain's charter to stderr at authoring time while leaving the piped next-id and --json stdout byte-identical, when a requirement is being authored.
requirements.why: The charter must reach the author without polluting the machine channel; any byte change on stdout would break every script parsing the next id.

requirements.statement: spec-engine/TAXONOMY.md presents the six headline invariants under their post-reorg target domains with a note that the exact successor requirement ids are reconciled during the Phase 2 reorg, when the invariants are catalogued.
requirements.why: Recording target domains now while deferring exact ids keeps the charter honest before the supersessions land, instead of inventing ids that Phase 2 will contradict.
